one_line_takeaway: TrapSuffix reframes suffix-jailbreak defense from passive detection to proactive optimization-landscape manipulation via LoRA fine-tuning, cutting average ASR to ~1% with near-zero inference overhead (15.87MB memory vs. 10^4MB for detector baselines) and 87.9% average traceability of the jailbreaks that do succeed, though it leaves a residual gap on Vicuna-13B and does not reliably suppress cross-model transfer attacks.
key_finding: TrapSuffix reduces average ASR across all 15 model/attack combinations to 0.01 (i.e., below 1%, stated in the abstract as 'below 0.01%' though Table 1 reports the aggregate as 0.01 on a 0-1 ASR scale), versus baselines ranging from 0.00 (WildGuard-Post) to 0.51 (GradSafe); no-defense baseline averages 0.53 ASR. It achieves this with near-zero inference overhead (tracing step measured at 0.000219s, reported as ≈0.0 in Table 1) and only 15.87 MB additional GPU memory, versus 1,839-29,013 MB for LLM-based/guard-model detection defenses. Average Tracing Success Rate (TSR) across the five attacks is 87.90% (109/124 successful jailbreaks traced), ranging from 100% (Transfer attack) down to 20% (Probe attack, attributed to Probe's use of a GPT-2 draft model altering the search distribution). Under an adaptive attacker with full (100%) knowledge and blacklisting of all trap suffixes, ASR remains 0.00 on a 25-prompt GCG subset, indicating the defense's effect is not solely dependent on the attacker unknowingly using trap tokens but on the ruggedness of the induced loss landscape. Increasing attacker optimization budget to 4,000 GCG iterations still yields 0% ASR. AutoDAN's FPR (non-refusal responses judged successful by the attacker but not actually harmful) rises to 100% on Qwen-2.5-7B and LLaMA-3-8B, and 96% on Vicuna-13B, under TrapSuffix versus much lower baseline rates. Ablation shows ASR suppression is robust to removing any single loss term (stays at 0.0%), but traceability (WithTraps rate) collapses from 24.0% (full method) to 0.0% when L1 or L4 is individually removed, and to 8.0-12.0% under various pairwise removals -- indicating traceability, unlike attack suppression, depends on the joint action of all four loss terms.
core_contribution: TrapSuffix, a lightweight LoRA fine-tuning defense against suffix-based LLM jailbreaks (e.g., GCG-style attacks that append optimized adversarial token suffixes to harmful questions). Instead of passively detecting suspicious suffixes, the defense proactively reshapes the model's adversarial-loss landscape so that suffix optimization is nudged into one of two defender-controlled outcomes: (1) convergence to defender-planted 'deceptive local minima' (trap suffixes) that fail to produce a working jailbreak, or (2) success only via suffixes that necessarily contain traceable trap-suffix fingerprints, enabling attribution. The method injects a designated set of trap suffixes (k=100) into the base model via four LoRA-trained loss terms: local optimality enforcement (hinge loss making trap suffixes locally optimal relative to random suffixes), trap safety regularization (elevating the adversarial loss floor for trap suffixes so local optimality doesn't equal jailbreak success), gradient-guided attraction (margin loss on token-embedding gradients steering the search toward trap tokens), and semantic termination control (making trap suffixes elicit non-refusal-but-harmless responses, exploiting attacks that stop once refusal language disappears). These are combined with a standard LM utility-preservation loss into one joint training objective, with all trap-specific terms implemented as LoRA adapters (rank r=8, lora_alpha=16, dropout 0.05, applied to q_proj/v_proj, lr 5e-5, 40 epochs) so the base model and inference pipeline are unmodified.
threat_model: Attacker: aims to induce an aligned LLM to generate harmful/policy-violating content by iteratively optimizing an adversarial suffix S appended to a harmful question Q (prompt P = Q⋄S); can repeatedly query the model, observe a surrogate loss/likelihood signal, freely choose suffix length/initialization/optimizer, and adapt strategy if aware of the deployed defense (i.e., adaptive/white-box-aware attacker is explicitly considered). Defender: has full offline access to model parameters and can fine-tune them, but cannot distinguish benign from adversarial queries at inference time and does not know which optimization algorithm/search strategy the attacker uses. Defense objective is formalized as three properties: Attack Resilience (expected jailbreak success below epsilon), Fingerprint Traceability (any successful jailbreak's suffix must exceed a traceability score threshold alpha), and Utility Preservation (benign task performance is not significantly degraded).
limitations: Vicuna-13B shows non-zero residual ASR under GCG (0.05) and Probe (0.04) attacks even with TrapSuffix, attributed by the authors to tokenizer differences requiring a different trap-suffix construction strategy for that model -- an acknowledged model-specific weak point rather than a fully solved case. Vicuna-13B also shows notably lower traceability scores than the other two models, again attributed to its tokenizer splitting trap suffixes into mismatched subword units, which undercuts confidence that the traceability score is a stable, tokenizer-agnostic measure. Utility preservation (Table 5) is only 'marginal' by the authors' own characterization but the numbers show some large swings: Vicuna-13B ARC-Challenge accuracy actually increases from 8.8% (base) to 26.2% (TrapSuffix) -- a change the paper does not explain, and which is a red flag for the stability/interpretability of the utility claims for that model. Cross-model transfer results (RQ4.2/Table 4) are mixed: transfer-attack ASR reductions from TrapSuffix are inconsistent across target families (e.g., meaningful average reductions transferring from LLaMA-3-8B, 12.75% on open-source and 4.33% on closed-source targets) but suffixes transferred from Qwen-2.5-7B or Vicuna-13B do not show consistent ASR reduction on other architectures, meaning the defense's protection does not reliably transfer to third-party or closed-source models even when the traceability fingerprint sometimes still appears. The defense requires the defender to pre-fine-tune the exact deployed model with knowledge of a private trap-suffix set; the paper does not discuss what happens if the trap-suffix set itself leaks beyond the partial-knowledge adaptive experiment, nor does it evaluate against suffix-search strategies not covered by the five tested attacks. All quantitative traceability/ablation robustness results (Tables 2, 3, 6) are computed on relatively small samples (25-124 jailbreak instances), which the paper does not accompany with confidence intervals or variance estimates.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 harmful prompts, OpenAI policy risk taxonomy)
Databricks Dolly-15K (instruction-response pairs for utility-preserving fine-tuning)
quantitative_results: Average ASR across 3 models x 5 attacks: 0.53 (no defense) -> 0.01 (TrapSuffix)
Average Tracing Success Rate (TSR): 87.90% (109/124 successful jailbreaks traced); per-attack TSR: GCG 44.44%, AutoDAN 75.00%, Probe 20.00%, Transfer 100.00%, JSAA 66.67%
Average additional GPU memory overhead: 15.87 MB (TrapSuffix) vs. 1,839-29,013 MB for baseline defenses (e.g., WildGuard-Pre/Post ~15,686 MB, SelfDefend-Direct ~28,924 MB)
Average inference delay: TrapSuffix ≈ 0.0s (tracing step measured at 0.000219s) vs. up to 38.83s for SmoothLLM-Pre
Adaptive attacker with 100% trap-suffix knowledge: ASR remains 0.00 on a 25-prompt GCG subset (Qwen-2.5-7B); ASR stays 0% up to 4,000 GCG optimization iterations

AutoDAN False Positive Rate under TrapSuffix: 100% (Qwen-2.5-7B), 100% (LLaMA-3-8B), 96% (Vicuna-13B), versus lower FPR without defense (39%, 61%, 49% respectively per Fig. 2 solid bars for no-defense ASR context)
Vicuna-13B residual ASR under TrapSuffix: GCG 0.05, Probe 0.04 (non-zero, attributed to tokenizer mismatch)

Utility (Table 5, base vs TrapSuffix): Qwen-2.5-7B ARC-C 82.6->78.4, HellaSwag 59.8->59.0, MMLU 71.76->70.22; LLaMA-3-8B ARC-C 70.0->76.2, HellaSwag 58.0->58.3, MMLU 64.72->64.02; Vicuna-13B ARC-C 8.8->26.2, HellaSwag 60.4->57.9, MMLU 54.55->50.37

Ablation (Table 6): full TrapSuffix WithTraps rate 24.0%; removing L1 or L4 individually drops WithTraps to 0.0%; removing L2 or L3 individually retains 4.0%; pairwise removals yield 8.0-12.0%; ASR stays at 0.0% under all single-term ablations

Cross-model transfer (Table 4): from LLaMA-3-8B source, TrapSuffix reduces average transfer ASR by 12.75% on open-source targets and 4.33% on closed-source targets; reductions inconsistent for Qwen-2.5-7B and Vicuna-13B sources

Base-vs-LoRA transferability (Fig. 6): suffixes optimized on base models transfer to LoRA-adapted variants of the same architecture at 0.0% ASR in all three model families (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B base->base 89.0%, base->LoRA 0.0%)
